facts.t: Truman relieves General Douglas MacArthur of his Korean War command, sparking national controversy.
J.D. Salinger publishes The Catcher in the Rye, an instant literary sensation.
The UNIVAC I, the first commercial computer in the US, is delivered to the Census Bureau.
I Love Lucy premieres on CBS, becoming one of television's most beloved sitcoms.
President Truman's speech becomes the first US broadcast carried live coast to coast on television.
Dwight D. Eisenhower is elected president in a landslide over Adlai Stevenson.
Mr. Potato Head becomes the first toy ever advertised on television.
The US tests Ivy Mike, the world's first full-scale hydrogen bomb, in the Marshall Islands.
Jonas Salk begins the first human trials of his polio vaccine.
No-Cal, marketed as the first diet soft drink, hits shelves in Brooklyn.
The Korean Armistice Agreement ends three years of fighting on the peninsula.
Julius and Ethel Rosenberg are executed for conspiracy to commit espionage.
Playboy magazine debuts with Marilyn Monroe on its first cover.
Chevrolet unveils the first Corvette, hand-built in Flint, Michigan.
Swanson introduces the frozen TV dinner, changing how America eats at home.
The Supreme Court rules unanimously in Brown v. Board of Education that school segregation is unconstitutional.
Elvis Presley releases his first single, That's All Right, on Sun Records.
The USS Nautilus, the world's first nuclear-powered submarine, is launched.
Ray Kroc signs a franchise agreement that will grow into the McDonald's empire.
Sports Illustrated hits newsstands with its very first issue.
Rosa Parks' arrest sparks the Montgomery bus boycott, a turning point for civil rights.
Disneyland opens its gates in Anaheim, California.
Jonas Salk's polio vaccine is declared safe and effective, ending a national nightmare.
Actor James Dean dies in a car crash at age 24, cementing his legend.
Ray Kroc opens his first McDonald's franchise in Des Plaines, Illinois.
Eisenhower signs the Federal-Aid Highway Act, creating the Interstate Highway System.
Elvis Presley's hip-shaking Ed Sullivan Show appearance scandalizes and thrills America.
In God We Trust is adopted as the official motto of the United States.
American actress Grace Kelly marries Prince Rainier III of Monaco.
TAT-1, the first transatlantic telephone cable, links the US and Europe.
The Soviet Union launches Sputnik 1, kicking off the Space Race and rattling America.
Nine Black students integrate Little Rock's Central High School under federal guard.
Wham-O begins selling the plastic flying disc it later trademarks as the Frisbee.
Dr. Seuss publishes The Cat in the Hat.
Jack Kerouac's On the Road defines the Beat Generation.
Congress creates NASA, launching America's civilian space program.
Explorer 1 becomes the first satellite launched by the United States.
Wham-O's Hula Hoop becomes an overnight fitness and toy craze.
Pizza Hut opens its first restaurant in Wichita, Kansas.
The Colts beat the Giants in sudden-death overtime in what's called The Greatest Game Ever Played.
Hawaii becomes the 50th state, completing the modern United States.
Alaska becomes the 49th state, the largest by far.
The Barbie doll debuts at the American International Toy Fair in New York.
Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and the Big Bopper die in a plane crash, the day the music died.
Fidel Castro's revolutionaries topple the Cuban government, reshaping US foreign policy.
John F. Kennedy narrowly defeats Richard Nixon to win the presidency.
Kennedy and Nixon face off in the first televised presidential debate.
The FDA approves the first oral contraceptive pill.
Harper Lee publishes To Kill a Mockingbird.
The Flintstones premieres, becoming the first prime-time animated sitcom.
John F. Kennedy is inaugurated, urging Americans to ask what you can do for your country.
The CIA-backed Bay of Pigs invasion of Cuba ends in a humiliating failure.
Alan Shepard becomes the first American in space aboard Freedom 7.
Freedom Riders board interstate buses to challenge segregation across the South.
Roger Maris hits his 61st home run, breaking Babe Ruth's single-season record.
The Cuban Missile Crisis brings the US and USSR to the brink of nuclear war.
John Glenn becomes the first American to orbit the Earth.
Sam Walton opens the first Walmart store in Rogers, Arkansas.
Johnny Carson takes over as host of The Tonight Show.
Rachel Carson publishes Silent Spring, igniting the modern environmental movement.
President John F. Kennedy is assassinated in Dallas, Texas.
Martin Luther King Jr. delivers his I Have a Dream speech at the March on Washington.
Betty Friedan's The Feminine Mystique helps launch second-wave feminism.
The US Postal Service introduces the five-digit ZIP Code.
TV networks air the first instant replay during an Army-Navy football broadcast.
President Johnson signs the Civil Rights Act of 1964 into law.
The Beatles' Ed Sullivan Show debut sets a still-record TV audience.
The Gulf of Tonkin incident leads to escalated US involvement in Vietnam.
Muhammad Ali, then Cassius Clay, stuns Sonny Liston to win the heavyweight title.
Congress declares bourbon whiskey a distinctive product of the United States.
President Johnson signs the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
US Marines land at Da Nang, marking the start of American ground combat in Vietnam.
Civil rights leader Malcolm X is assassinated in Harlem.
A cascading grid failure causes the Great Northeast Blackout, darkening 30 million people.
Ralph Nader's Unsafe at Any Speed exposes the auto industry and spurs safety laws.
The Supreme Court's Miranda v. Arizona ruling requires police to inform suspects of their rights.
Star Trek premieres on NBC, launching a science-fiction phenomenon.
Betty Friedan helps found the National Organization for Women.
The campy Batman TV series debuts, becoming an instant pop-culture hit.
Medicare begins covering health care for millions of older Americans.
Thurgood Marshall is sworn in as the first Black justice on the US Supreme Court.
The Green Bay Packers beat the Kansas City Chiefs in the first Super Bowl.
The Summer of Love draws thousands of young people to San Francisco.
The Supreme Court's Loving v. Virginia ruling strikes down bans on interracial marriage.
Rolling Stone magazine publishes its first issue.
Martin Luther King Jr. is assassinated in Memphis, Tennessee.
Senator Robert F. Kennedy is assassinated after winning the California primary.
Apollo 8 astronauts become the first humans to orbit the Moon.
Chaotic protests erupt outside the Democratic National Convention in Chicago.
Intel is founded, soon becoming a cornerstone of the computing industry.
Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin become the first humans on the Moon.
Half a million people gather for the Woodstock music festival in upstate New York.
The Stonewall riots in Greenwich Village galvanize the LGBT rights movement.
The first message is sent over ARPANET, a forerunner of the internet.
Sesame Street premieres on public television.
National Guard troops kill four students at an anti-war protest at Kent State University.
Twenty million Americans mark the first Earth Day.
Apollo 13's crew survives an onboard explosion in NASA's successful failure.
The Beatles officially announce their breakup.
ABC premieres Monday Night Football, changing how America watches the NFL.
The 26th Amendment lowers the voting age to 18 nationwide.
State troopers retake Attica Correctional Facility after a deadly prison uprising.
Walt Disney World opens near Orlando, Florida.
The New York Times begins publishing the leaked Pentagon Papers.
Starbucks opens its first store in Seattle's Pike Place Market.
Burglars tied to Nixon's campaign are caught at the Watergate complex.
Nixon becomes the first sitting US president to visit Communist China.
Title IX bans sex discrimination in federally funded education, transforming women's sports.
Atari releases Pong, helping launch the video game industry.
The Godfather premieres in theaters, redefining American cinema.
The Supreme Court's Roe v. Wade ruling establishes a constitutional right to abortion.
The Paris Peace Accords formally end direct US military involvement in Vietnam.
American Indian Movement activists occupy Wounded Knee, South Dakota, for 71 days.
Secretariat wins the Triple Crown by a record 31 lengths at the Belmont Stakes.
Chicago's Sears Tower is completed as the world's tallest building.
Richard Nixon resigns the presidency amid the Watergate scandal.
Hank Aaron breaks Babe Ruth's career home run record.
Newspaper heiress Patty Hearst is kidnapped by the Symbionese Liberation Army.
A pack of Wrigley's gum becomes the first product scanned with a UPC barcode.
People magazine launches its first issue.
The fall of Saigon marks the end of the Vietnam War.
Bill Gates and Paul Allen found Microsoft.
Jaws terrifies audiences and becomes Hollywood's first summer blockbuster.
Saturday Night Live premieres on NBC.
Miller Lite launches nationally, popularizing the American light beer category.
